food_name: Asam Pedas Ikan (sour spicy fish stew)
region_of_origin: Malay Peninsula — ubiquitous in Singapore and Malaysia Malay cuisine

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Asam Pedas Ikan. Closest parent is tamarind-based spiced fish stew.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish stew in tamarind-based spiced broth (Malay asam pedas style)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard Asam Pedas Ikan serving is 300g of fish with broth: approximately 150g fish fillet/piece plus 150g sour-spicy tamarind broth. Typically served alongside steamed rice (not included in these values).

nutrient_source.title: Asam Pedas Ikan — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD both have entries for Asam Pedas fish stew. Values per 100g are for the broth-only preparation without steamed rice. Fish type: primary reference uses stingray (ikan pari) as the canonical Singapore hawker version, though snapper and mackerel are also used. Tamarind contributes tartness and moderate acidity noted in the values.

gi_source: GI not applicable — Asam Pedas Ikan is a fish and broth dish with minimal carbohydrate content (4.2g/100g). The tamarind contributes organic acids but negligible glycaemically relevant carbohydrate. Consumed with steamed rice which carries the glycaemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from belacan (shrimp paste), fish sauce or anchovies in the spice paste, and salt. A 300g serving delivers approximately 1,560mg sodium — 65% of the 2,400mg daily limit. Significantly lower sodium than dry curries (rendang) or heavily seasoned dishes, but notable for hypertension patients.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension can enjoy asam pedas ikan with portion control. Request reduced belacan or salt in the preparation. Consume with steamed vegetables rather than salt-seasoned sides. Limit to 1 portion per meal with a low-sodium accompaniment.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Asam Pedas Ikan is a protein-dominant dish with negligible carbohydrate (4.2g/100g). The dish itself has minimal glycaemic impact — the carbohydrate load comes from accompanying steamed rice. Fish protein supports insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should view asam pedas ikan as an excellent protein and omega-3 fatty acid source. The dish has minimal direct glycaemic impact. Pair with controlled rice portions (100g steamed rice maximum) and add a vegetable side to achieve a balanced, lower-GL meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (1.1g/100g including EPA and DHA equivalents from marine fish). Turmeric, galangal, and lemongrass in the spice base contribute anti-inflammatory compounds. Tamarind provides vitamin C.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Asam Pedas Ikan is an excellent choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — omega-3 from fish reduces joint inflammation, and the anti-inflammatory spice base provides additional benefit. Recommend 2–3 times per week. Choose mackerel (higher omega-3) as the fish type where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Asam Pedas Ikan is a quintessential Malay sour-spicy fish stew that represents one of the most beloved flavour profiles in Malay cuisine. 'Asam' means sour (from tamarind) and 'pedas' means spicy (from dried chillis). The fish — most commonly stingray, red snapper, or mackerel — is simmered in a broth made from tamarind water, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, dried chilli, and toasted belacan (shrimp paste). The signature ingredient is torch ginger flower (bunga kantan), which adds a distinctive floral-spicy aroma. Unlike Indian fish curries which use coconut milk, asam pedas has a clear, sour, oil-light broth. It is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with excellent omega-3 fatty acid content.

confidence_reason: Direct entries in both HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD for asam pedas fish stew. Values confirmed across both sources with minor variation in sodium values (primary sodium source: belacan concentration).
notes: Values are for the fish and broth component only — steamed rice accompaniment not included. Fish type affects protein and omega-3 values: stingray has slightly higher fat than mackerel; mackerel has higher omega-3. Vitamin B12 content (1.8mcg/100g) is significant from marine fish. Key allergens: fish and crustaceans (belacan). Tamarind provides organic acids (tartaric, malic) that contribute to the characteristic sour flavour and the dish's moderate antioxidant capacity.
